title: Breitmann As An Uhlan. III. Breitmann and Bouilli
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Trן¿½s estimן¿½ ami, Ick seyn nock nit verdorb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vielleickt Sie denck wohl kar, das ick sey tod gestorb,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ock ne Kott loben Danck, ick leb nock kanss wohl auf.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Naturlich wie Kespenst die off die Kasse keh.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ - Deutsch-Franzos, Leipzig, 1736.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot roombles down de Bergstrass?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot a grash ish in de air!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mit a desberate gonfusion,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und a gry of wild tespair,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Das sind gethrן¿½sht Franzosen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und dose who after flee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Are de terror of Champagner,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Die Uhlan cavallrie.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So liddle say die hoonted,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De hoonters lesser shdill;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Frank is ride forן¿½s leben,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Deutscher rides to kill.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ofer dickly-doosty faces
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Deir eyes like wild-katzs glare;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ De blut und iron ridin
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of furie und despair.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Boot of all de wild Uhlanen,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Breitmann ride de pest;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For he mark de Frן¿½ntsch gommanter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ish most elegandtly tresst.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und ash he coom down on him,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dereן¿½s a deatן¿½ look in his eye:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Gotts! if I carfe dat toorkey,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How Iן¿½ll make de stoofin vly!ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mit a clotter und a flotter
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like a hell-sturm dey are on:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mit a rottle to de pattle
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Coom de Deutschers, knockinן¿½ down,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Down de moundain to a bruckן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vhy die Frן¿½ntschmen toorn ad bay?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oder Deutsch were dere pefore dem,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und die pridge ish coot avay!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Von second der Franzose
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Look down mit blitzen eye;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Von second at de bruckן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Den toorn him round to die.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vhile mit out-ge-poke-te lanze,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like ter teufel shot from hell,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Rode der ploonder-shtarvin Breitmann
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On der grau-bart Colonel.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot for der Coptain Breitmann
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ish shdop in his career?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot for he pool his pridle?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot for let down his speer?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot for his eyes like saucers
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grow pigger, rimmed mit staub?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Vot for his hair, a pristlin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Lift oop his pickel-haub?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So awfool so oneartן¿½ly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So treadful was his glare,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ So unbeschreiblich gastly,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dat der Colonel self was shkare.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Oop come der Breitmann ridin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und mit gratin force he said:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Bist du wirkelich lebendig?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Can de grafe gife oop its tead?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Dou livest yet dou breafן¿½st yet,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dough oldter now you pe
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since I mordered you in Strasburg,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mein freund mon Jean Bouilli.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We lofed de selfe maiden
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wohl forty years agone:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She died to hear I kilt you:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Jean how weiss your beard ish grown!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½I would gife my Habן¿½ und Gן¿½ter,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dereto mein bit of life
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Couldt I pring dat shild to leben,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und make her, Jean, dy wife!ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Here der Breitmann boorst out gryin,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Like a liddle prook vept he;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und dey hugged and gissed einander,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Breitmann und Bouilli.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Ach, de efils dat from efil
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Troo a life ish efer grow!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had I nefer dink I killed you,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Many a man were livin now-
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Many a man dat shleeps in cane-brakes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Many a man py pillow-shore;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For dy morder mate me reckelos,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Und von tead man gries for more!

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O Mן¿½dchen! schן¿½n im Himmel!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (Warst schon on eartן¿½ difine)
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Canן¿½st dink among de Engeln
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of soosh as me und mine?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Den look on soosh a Reue,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ash eartן¿½ has nefer known:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Whereto hast dou a sabre?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Wherefore not kill me, Jean?ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½O, ne pleurez pas, mon Breitmann!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Je trouve cela trop fort,ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Gry der Colonel sehr politely;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½How! you crois dat I was mort!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mon Dieu! ן¿½Tis but one minute,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As we galloped to this plain,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I thought your spear, mon gaillard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Would kill me oן¿½er again.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Je vous fais mon compliment,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Your tendresse becomes you well;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Et ne pleurez pas, mon brave,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Pour la petite demoiselle.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have had a thousand since;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One can always find such game;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Et pour dire la vן¿½ritן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I have quite forgot her name.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Der Breitmann lok so earnest,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Long and earnest at his foe,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ash if seein troo his augen
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To de forty years ago.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Mit vot a shmile der Breitmann
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Toorned roundt und rode away:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dat was all his parting greetin
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To der Cן¿½lonן¿½l Franן¿½ais.
meaning:
This evocative piece by Charles G. Leland represents a masterful exploration of classic. The lines capture a profound emotional resonance, inviting the reader to reflect on the deeper themes of human experience and artistic expression.

In the broader context of Charles G. Leland's bibliography, these specific lines stand out for their clarity and poignancy. Whether you are searching for inspiration or a moment of quiet contemplation, this work offers a timeless perspective that transcends its original era.

At Linespedia, we have curated this specific poem to ensure it reaches a modern audience. We recommend sharing these lines as a digital poster or using them as a thoughtful caption to connect with others who appreciate the enduring beauty of verse.
